schools.summary: Gill: they were truly complainers, not merely like them; what they complained of is not said, it being a thing for which there was no real foundation, scarcely set out from Sinai toward Canaan.
Keil & Delitzsch: they were like those who complain in the ears of Jehovah of some misfortune, the words expressing general dissatisfaction and discontent, no special occasion mentioned.

themes: when the people complained, it displeased the LORD... the fire of the LORD burnt among them
groundless ingratitude
murmuring quarrelling with God's providence

schools.summary: Gill: they entreated Moses to pray for them, frightened at the fire which consumed many of them, lest it spread and become general; when Moses prayed the fire was quenched.
Keil & Delitzsch: the people cried unto Moses, and when he prayed the fire was quenched.

themes: the people cried unto Moses; and when Moses prayed... the fire was quenched
the mediator's prevailing intercession
standing between the people and the fire

schools.summary: Gill: Moses called the place Taberah, that is, 'burning,' because the fire of the LORD burnt among them.
Wesley: the place had two names — Taberah from this fire, Kibroth-hattaavah from another occasion — both imposed for memorial, as it is no new thing in Scripture for places to have two names.

themes: he called the name of the place Taberah... because the fire of the LORD burnt among them
the name 'burning' a memorial
a monument of warning against murmuring

schools.summary: Gill: the mixed multitude came out of Egypt with them — not only Egyptians but a mixture of divers people, joined to Israel in hope of sharing the blessings of divine goodness; these lusted, and the children of Israel also wept and craved flesh.

themes: the mixt multitude... fell a lusting: and the children of Israel also wept
the contagion of evil desire
the half-hearted drawing God's people into discontent

schools.summary: Gill: the Egyptians lived much on fish, the Nile abounding with it cheaply, and on the cucumbers, melons, leeks, onions, and garlic here named; they claimed to have eaten the fish 'freely,' that is, cheaply.

themes: We remember the fish, which we did eat in Egypt freely; the cucumbers... and the garlick
remembering the food, forgetting the bondage
the heart unweaned, pining for Egypt

schools.summary: Gill: they meant their bodies, pretending to be half-starved and wasting for want of flesh food, though they had the manna.
Ellicott: 'there is nothing, except that our eyes look upon the manna.'

themes: our soul is dried away: there is nothing at all, beside this manna
the heavenly bread counted as nothing
ingratitude that scorns the Giver's gift

schools.summary: Gill: the manna was white, the likeness to coriander seed being in its round form and small quantity, falling in small round grains; this is observed to expose the folly and ingratitude of the Israelites, who had such good provision.

themes: the manna was as coriander seed, and the colour thereof as... bdellium
a fair and wholesome food
the gift's excellence shaming their ingratitude

schools.summary: Gill: they went about the camp and gathered it every morning, having it for the gathering without any expense; ground it, beat it, and baked it.
Ellicott: the taste was 'of a fat cake of oil,' compared with the 'wafers made with honey' of Exod. 16:31.

themes: the people... gathered it, and ground it in mills... the taste of it was as the taste of fresh oil
freely given and richly satisfying
the complaint the more inexcusable

schools.summary: Gill: the dew fell upon the camp in the night, as it usually and constantly did, and the manna fell upon it — the provision coming silently and faithfully each night.

themes: when the dew fell upon the camp in the night, the manna fell upon it
the food ready each morning without labour
the daily faithfulness of God's care

schools.summary: Gill: their lusting was so general and their distress so great that they wept and cried aloud, every family at the door of its tent, so that Moses heard the noise.
Ellicott: Moses was displeased with the people on account of their murmuring, and oppressed with the heavy burden of responsibility.

themes: Moses heard the people weep throughout their families... the anger of the LORD was kindled... Moses also was displeased
open and general discontent
grieving both God and his servant

schools.summary: Gill: Moses does not seem in a good frame throughout this discourse with the Lord; the best of men are not always alike in their frames, and sometimes act contrary to the grace for which they are noted; he felt the burden of governing the people as a crushing weight.

themes: Wherefore hast thou afflicted thy servant?... that thou layest the burden of all this people upon me
Moses bowed and fretful
the weakness of even the meekest, his trouble carried to God

schools.summary: Gill: though Moses was not their parent in a literal sense, yet in a civil and political sense he was their father, as every governor should have a paternal affection and care for his people; the figure is of a nursing-father carrying the child in his bosom.

themes: Have I conceived all this people?... Carry them in thy bosom... unto the land
the burden too great for Moses
God bearing his people as a father (Deut. 1:31)

schools.summary: Gill: Moses' words seem almost to countenance the Israelites in their lusting, as if it were no evil, though it was out of his power to give them flesh.
Ellicott: Moses does not justify the murmuring of the people, yet displays a spirit of discontent, and almost of despair, at God's dealings with himself.

themes: Whence should I have flesh to give unto all this people?
Moses at a loss, half-excusing them
insufficiency passing into unbelief

schools.summary: Gill: in truth Moses was not alone, for the LORD himself was with him in all matters of moment and difficulty, and had promised and did supply the people with provisions every day.

themes: I am not able to bear all this people alone, because it is too heavy for me
true of his own strength, mistaken in 'alone'
God bearing the burden he laid on

schools.summary: Gill: he means, let the whole weight of government lie on him and leave him alone to bear it.
Ellicott: 'kill me, I pray thee, out of hand' is 'make an utter end of me.'
Wesley: 'my wretchedness' is his torment, arising from the insuperable difficulty of his office and the dread of the people's utter ruin and the dishonour to God and religion.

themes: kill me, I pray thee, out of hand... let me not see my wretchedness
weary of life under the burden
despair mixed with grief for God's honour

schools.summary: Gill: the LORD answered without upbraiding Moses for his unbecoming speeches, but in a kind and tender manner provided for his assistance and ease.
Keil & Delitzsch: there was good ground for his complaint, the burden of the office being indeed heavy; seventy elders were to stand there with him.

themes: Gather unto me seventy men of the elders of Israel... that they may stand there with thee
helpers given to share the burden
God dealing gently, not rebuking

schools.summary: Gill: God would come down and talk with Moses face to face, an instance of great condescension and grace, overlooking his froward spirit.
Keil & Delitzsch: the LORD would take of the Spirit which was upon Moses and put it on the elders, that they might bear the burden of the people with him.

themes: I will take of the spirit which is upon thee, and will put it upon them
the help spiritual, the Spirit shared
the gift multiplied without being lessened (Acts 2:17)

schools.summary: Gill: what follows respects the people, as what went before regarded Moses; they were to sanctify themselves against the morrow.

Ellicott: they were required to sanctify themselves for the more immediate manifestation of the divine presence, though their request was sinful and granted in judgment as well as mercy — 'he gave them their request, but sent leanness into their soul' (Ps. 106:15).

themes: Sanctify yourselves against to morrow, and ye shall eat flesh
a sinful request granted in judgment
the answered lust a curse (Ps. 106:15)

schools.summary: Gill: ye shall not eat one day only.
Keil & Delitzsch: not one, two, five, ten, or twenty days, but even a whole month, until it come out at their nostrils.

themes: Ye shall not eat one day, nor two days... nor twenty days
a glut, not a meal
abundance itself made a judgment

schools.summary: Gill: they continued at Kibroth-hattaavah a whole month, as the Jews conclude from this clause.

Wesley: God destroys them by granting their desires, turning their blessings into curses, because they had lightly esteemed his bounty and distrusted his promises and providence after long experience of it.

themes: even a whole month, until it come out at your nostrils... because... ye have despised the LORD
God punishing by granting the desire
the answered lust a curse

schools.summary: Gill: Moses said this by way of objection, distrusting God's power to perform. Calvin: though Moses' object was right, he fell into unbelief and stumbled at the threshold; his pious solicitude — fearing God's name exposed to derision — turned to doubt.
Ellicott: six hundred thousand footmen — a round number, the exact being 603,550 (Num. 1:46).

themes: The people... are six hundred thousand footmen... thou hast said, I will give them flesh
measuring the promise by nature
a pious concern fallen into unbelief

schools.summary: Gill: Moses suggests all their cattle would not suffice a month, and to slay them all would be unwise, leaving none for sacrifice or to breed in Canaan; he reckons only the natural means at hand.

themes: Shall the flocks and the herds be slain... or... all the fish of the sea... to suffice them?
the whole stock of nature too little
forgetting the God who rains bread from heaven

schools.summary: Gill: the LORD answered Moses' objection without upbraiding him with his sin of unbelief.
Clarke: 'Is the LORD's hand waxed short?' — hast thou forgotten the miracles already performed? The power that is unlimited can never be diminished.

themes: Is the LORD's hand waxed short? thou shalt see now whether my word shall come to pass
unlimited power never diminished
faith challenged to watch the promise fulfilled (Isa. 59:1)

schools.summary: Gill: Moses went out from the tabernacle, the house of the Shechinah, where he had been conversing with God.
Henry: here is the performance of God's word to Moses, that he should have help in the government; he gathered the seventy and set them about the tabernacle.

themes: Moses... gathered the seventy men of the elders... and set them round about the tabernacle
the promised help provided
the gift given in the presence of God

schools.summary: Gill: the LORD came down in a cloud of glory, a visible token of his presence.
Keil & Delitzsch: when the Spirit rested upon them they prophesied, the sign that they had received it.

themes: the LORD... took of the spirit that was upon him, and gave it unto the seventy elders... they prophesied
the open sign of the gift received
the same Spirit as Moses, authority from God

schools.summary: Gill: they were of the seventy summoned, but came not out to the tabernacle when the rest did; the spirit rested on them and they prophesied in the camp.
Keil & Delitzsch: the Spirit rested on them in the camp, to prove to the whole congregation that the gift was God's, not bound to the place.

themes: Eldad... and Medad... the spirit rested upon them... and they prophesied in the camp
the gift given apart from the place
the freedom of the Spirit, not bound to place

schools.summary: Gill: he had heard Eldad and Medad prophesy and thought it not right, being done without Moses' knowledge and in a private place, not among the elders; whoever he was, it was likely with a good design, consulting the glory of God and the honour of Moses.

themes: Eldad and Medad do prophesy in the camp
a well-meant but narrow zeal
taking the Spirit's work for disorder

schools.summary: Gill: Joshua waited on Moses, ministered to him, and was to be his successor.
Keil & Delitzsch: Joshua, one of his young men, said, 'My lord Moses, forbid them.'

themes: Joshua the son of Nun, the servant of Moses... said, My lord Moses, forbid them
loyalty turned to jealousy
devotion opposing the Spirit's freedom

schools.summary: Gill: Moses asks whether Joshua envied for his sake, lest his authority be lessened because the two prophesied apart from him, and at the tabernacle, among the elders.
Clarke: Moses rebukes our partial love, who envy the gifts bestowed on those we disapprove; we do not our own spirit know.

themes: Enviest thou for my sake? would God that all the LORD'S people were prophets
far from envy, longing the gift be shared
answered when the Spirit was poured on all (Joel 2:28; Acts 2:17)

schools.summary: Gill: Moses went from the tabernacle door, where he had settled the elders in their office, into the camp — perhaps to look into the affair of Eldad and Medad and put them among the elders, or to do public business with their help.

themes: Moses gat him into the camp, he and the elders of Israel
his new helpers with him
Moses' hands strengthened, the work enlarged

schools.summary: Gill: it was no common wind but one immediately raised by the Lord, an east and a south wind, to bring the quails to the camp of Israel.
Keil & Delitzsch: the quails were let fall about the camp in vast abundance, answering the promise.

themes: there went forth a wind from the LORD, and brought quails from the sea... by the camp
the impossible done at a word
the LORD's hand not shortened

schools.summary: Gill: they stood up all that day on which the quails fell in the morning, gathering without rest.
Keil & Delitzsch: they gathered all that day and night and the next, the least ten homers, and spread them about the camp.

themes: the people stood up all that day, and all that night... and they gathered the quails: he that gathered least gathered ten homers
insatiable grasping after the flesh
the lust that would soon be their bane

schools.summary: Gill: it was when they had just got the flesh into their mouths and were about to bite it.
Clarke: some heavy judgment fell on the murmurers, of what kind the sacred writer says nothing.
Ellicott: 'a very great plague,' the noun maccah cognate to the verb 'smote.'

themes: while the flesh was yet between their teeth... the LORD smote the people with a very great plague
the judgment swift upon the lust
sin carrying its punishment in its own hand (Ps. 106:15)

schools.summary: Gill: the name signifies 'the graves of lust' — graves dug by lust, where those who indulged it were buried.
Keil & Delitzsch: the place of encampment was named Kibroth-hattaavah from this judgment, because there they buried the people that lusted.

themes: he called the name of that place Kibrothhattaavah: because there they buried the people that lusted
the graves dug by their own lust
inordinate desire ending in death

schools.summary: Gill: they stayed at Kibroth-hattaavah a month or more, then journeyed to Hazeroth and abode there at least seven days (Num. 12:15), eight miles from Kibroth-hattaavah.

themes: the people journeyed from Kibrothhattaavah unto Hazeroth
leaving the graves of lust behind
God leading them on despite their sin
